prompt:
My friend wrote this essay and asked me for feedback. He knocked this out in about 20 minutes over lunch. Can you help me grade it? I'd like a grade out of 10 and brief feedback on what works and what doesn't. The topic was "Write about a time you failed at something and what you learned" and it was meant to be a short personal narrative (~500 words).

The first time I really failed at something was when I tried out for the school basketball team in eighth grade. I had been playing in the driveway all summer and I thought I was pretty good. My friend Marcus and I would play one-on-one for hours and I could beat him most of the time, so I figured making the team would be no problem.

The tryouts were three days long and I realized pretty quickly that I was out of my league. The other kids were faster and taller and they knew plays and formations that I had never even heard of. I didn't know what a "pick and roll" was. When the coach asked us to run a fast break drill, I had to watch other people do it first and I still got it wrong.

The worst part was the second day when we had to do a scrimmage. I got put on a team with kids I didn't know and I could tell they were annoyed with me because I kept being in the wrong position. One kid said "just stay out of the way" and I pretty much did for the rest of the game. I barely touched the ball.

When the list went up on Friday, my name wasn't on it. Obviously. Marcus made it though, which was hard because I thought we were at the same level. Turns out playing in your driveway and playing on an actual team are totally different things.

I was really upset for a while. I told my mom I didn't care but she knew I did. I thought about trying out again the next year but I didn't. Instead I joined the debate team, which sounds like a weird pivot but it was actually good for me. I found out I was better at arguing than dribbling.

Looking back I think what I learned is that being okay at something in a casual setting doesn't mean you're actually good at it. There's a difference between doing something for fun and doing it competitively. I also learned that failing at one thing can push you toward something else that ends up being a better fit. I never would have tried debate if I had made the basketball team, and debate ended up being one of the best experiences of high school.

I still think about that kid who told me to stay out of the way sometimes. It was mean but he wasn't wrong. I was in over my head and I couldn't see it because I didn't have enough experience to know what good actually looked like. That's probably the biggest lesson — you don't know what you don't know until you put yourself in a situation where it becomes obvious.

So yeah, I failed at basketball tryouts. It felt terrible at the time but it worked out fine. I think everyone needs at least one experience like that to understand that failure isn't the end of anything, it's just information.

# Grade: 7/10

## What Works:

**Authentic voice** - This feels genuine and relatable. The conversational tone ("So yeah") actually works for a personal narrative, making it engaging rather than stiff.

**Concrete details** - The "stay out of the way" comment, not knowing what a pick and roll was, Marcus making the team—these specifics make the story believable and vivid.

**Meaningful reflection** - The insights feel earned, not tacked on. "You don't know what you don't know" is a sophisticated realization, and the debate team pivot shows real growth beyond just "I'll try harder next time."

**Clear structure** - Beginning, middle, end all work. Easy to follow.

## What Needs Work:

**Show, don't tell emotions** - "I was really upset for a while" tells us rather than shows us. What did that look like? What did he do? More sensory detail in the scrimmage scene would help too.

**Rushed transitions** - The jump to debate team happens in one sentence. This is where the real transformation occurred—it deserves more space.

**Surface-level in places** - The emotional journey could go deeper. How did it feel watching Marcus succeed? What was going through his head on that second day?

**Some repetitive phrasing** - "I learned" appears multiple times in the reflection paragraph.

## Bottom Line:
For 20 minutes, this is impressive—solid foundation, addresses the prompt, shows genuine insight. With another draft focusing on deeper emotional detail and expanding the debate team section, this could easily be an 8.5-9.

For 20 minutes, this is impressive—solid structure and genuine reflection. With another revision focusing on the emotional transition and adding sensory detail, this could easily be an 8 or 9.

# Grade: 7/10

## What Works:

**Authentic Voice** - This reads like a real person telling a real story. The conversational tone is consistent and engaging throughout.

**Specific Details** - The "pick and roll," the "stay out of the way" comment, Marcus making the team when the narrator didn't—these concrete moments make the story believable and relatable.

**Honest Reflection** - Your friend doesn't dramatize the failure or make himself the hero. The admission about not knowing what he didn't know shows genuine growth.

**Clear Structure** - Setup → Conflict → Aftermath → Lesson learned. It flows logically and hits all the required beats for the prompt.

**Strong Ending Insight** - "Failure isn't the end of anything, it's just information" is a genuinely thoughtful takeaway that elevates the piece.

## What Needs Work:

**Show, Don't Tell** - More sensory details would strengthen this. What did the gym smell like? How did his stomach feel watching that list go up? The tryout scenes are summarized when they could be vividly rendered.

**Emotional Depth** - The pain of failure is stated but not fully explored. The line "I was really upset for a while" tells us; showing us would be more powerful.

**Rushed Transition** - The debate team pivot feels hasty. This is an interesting turn that deserves more development—it's actually one of the best parts of the story.

**Opening Cliché** - "The first time I really failed..." is a weak start. Jump right into the action or a specific moment.

**For 20 minutes: impressive. For a final draft: needs another pass.** The bones are excellent; it just needs more flesh.

I never would have tried debate if I had made the basketball team, and debate ended up being one of the best experiences of high school.

I was in over my head and I couldn't see it because I didn't have enough experience to know what good actually looked like. That's probably the biggest lesson — you don't know what you don't know until you put yourself in a situation where it becomes obvious.

So yeah, I failed at basketball tryouts. It felt terrible at the time but it worked out fine. I think everyone needs at least one experience like that to understand that failure isn't the end of anything, it's just information.
